content:
# Joshua Chapter 12 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), page 236 | **Source Image:** `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_012_page_001.jpg`

---

*[The Tally of the Conquered — Twenty-four verses of archive. After two chapters of warfare — the southern campaign (ch. 10) and the northern campaign (ch. 11) — the narrator pauses and takes inventory. This is the official record: every king who fell, every throne that was emptied. The chapter splits in two: first the east side of the Jordan under Moses (vv. 1-6), then the west side under Joshua (vv. 7-24). The king-list in verses 9-24 is written in a distinctive staccato — king, city, one; king, city, one — thirty-one entries hammered out like a roll call of the defeated. The repetition is the rhetoric. This is not narrative but ledger, and the ledger speaks for itself.]*

---

**1** These are the kings of the land whom the Sons of Israel defeated. They took possession of their territory on the east side of the Jordan — from the Arnon Gorge all the way to Mount Hermon, including the entire eastern rift valley:

**2** Sihon, king of the Amorites. He ruled from Heshbon. His territory ran from Aroer on the rim of the Arnon Gorge — including the middle of the gorge itself — through half of Gilead as far as the Jabbok Gorge, which marks the Ammonite border.

**3** He also controlled the rift valley eastward to the Sea of Kinnereth, and southward through the rift valley to the Salt Sea, toward Beth-Jeshimoth, and on the south below the slopes of Pisgah.

**4** Then there was Og, king of Bashan — one of the last of the Rephaim. He ruled from Ashtaroth and Edrei,

**5** and held Mount Hermon, Salecah, and all of Bashan as far as the border of the Geshurites and the Maacathites. He also held half of Gilead, up to the territory of Sihon king of Heshbon.

**6** Moses, the servant of YHWH, along with the Sons of Israel, defeated these two. And Moses the servant of YHWH gave their land as a possession to the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh.

**7** And these are the kings of the land whom Joshua and the Sons of Israel defeated on the west side of the Jordan — from Baal-Gad in the Lebanon Valley all the way to Mount Halak, which rises toward Seir. Joshua gave this territory to the tribes of Israel as their possession, divided by tribe —

**8** in the hill country, the foothills, the rift valley, the mountain slopes, the wilderness, and the Negev — the land of the Hittites, the Amorites, the Canaanites, the Perizzites, the Hivites, and the Jebusites:

**9** The king of Jericho — one.
The king of Ai (near Bethel) — one.

**10** The king of Jerusalem — one.
The king of Hebron — one.

**11** The king of Jarmuth — one.
The king of Lachish — one.

**12** The king of Eglon — one.
The king of Gezer — one.

**13** The king of Debir — one.
The king of Geder — one.

**14** The king of Hormah — one.
The king of Arad — one.

**15** The king of Libnah — one.
The king of Adullam — one.

**16** The king of Makkedah — one.
The king of Bethel — one.

**17** The king of Tappuah — one.
The king of Hepher — one.

**18** The king of Aphek — one.
The king of Lasharon — one.

**19** The king of Madon — one.
The king of Hazor — one.

**20** The king of Shimron-Meron — one.
The king of Achshaph — one.

**21** The king of Taanach — one.
The king of Megiddo — one.

**22** The king of Kedesh — one.
The king of Jokneam in Carmel — one.

**23** The king of Dor (in the heights of Dor) — one.
The king of Goiim at Gilgal — one.

**24** The king of Tirzah — one.

All the kings: thirty-one.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Structure — The chapter as archive:** Joshua 12 is not narrative. It is a record — a bureaucratic summary closing the conquest section of the book (chapters 1-12) before the land-distribution section begins (chapters 13-21). The two-part structure (Moses' conquests east of the Jordan, then Joshua's conquests west of it) frames the entire military campaign as a single, continuous act of YHWH's faithfulness stretching back to Moses.

**V. 1 — "Arnon Gorge" (nachal 'Arnon):** The Hebrew nachal means a seasonal watercourse, a wadi — a steep-cut gorge that runs with water in the rainy season and lies dry the rest of the year. PNEUMA uses "gorge" rather than "wadi" or "brook" to convey the dramatic terrain to English readers. The Arnon cuts a deep canyon into the landscape east of the Salt Sea. The same rendering is used for the Jabbok in v. 2.

**V. 1 — "Rift valley" (ha-'Aravah):** The Arabah is the great geological rift running from the Sea of Kinnereth through the Salt Sea and south to the Gulf of Aqaba. PNEUMA renders it as "rift valley" for clarity in English — the term captures both the geography and the scale. The word appears in vv. 1, 3, and 8.

**V. 3 — "Sea of Kinnereth":** The ancient Hebrew name for the body of water later known as the Sea of Galilee. The name may derive from kinnor (harp), reflecting the lake's shape. PNEUMA preserves the Hebrew name rather than substituting a later designation.

**V. 3 — "Salt Sea":** The Hebrew name for the Dead Sea. PNEUMA follows the text rather than the modern name, which is not found in the Hebrew Bible.

**V. 3 — "Slopes of Pisgah" ('ashdot ha-Pisgah):** The Hebrew 'ashdot means "poured-out places" — slopes or watershed ridges descending from the Pisgah heights toward the rift valley. This is the same mountain range from which Moses viewed the promised land before his death (Deuteronomy 34:1).

**V. 4 — "One of the last of the Rephaim" (mi-yeter ha-Repha'im):** Literally "from the remnant of the Rephaim." The Rephaim were an ancient people of legendary stature. By the time of the conquest, they were nearly extinct — Og was among the final survivors. PNEUMA renders "one of the last" to capture the sense of a dying race. The LXX Vaticanus translates as "giants" (giganton), interpreting rather than transliterating.

**V. 7 — "Mount Halak, which rises toward Seir" (ha-har he-khalaq ha-'oleh Se'ir):** Hebrew khalaq means "smooth" or "bare" — a treeless, exposed mountain marking the southern boundary of Joshua's conquests. "Rises toward" captures the participial form: the mountain ascends in the direction of Seir (Edom).

**V. 8 — "Foothills" (ha-Shephelah):** The Shephelah is the low rolling hill country between the Judean highlands and the coastal plain — strategically vital, agriculturally rich. PNEUMA renders "foothills" as the most natural English equivalent, since the term is not widely known outside biblical scholarship.

**Vv. 9-24 — The king-list and the word "one" ('ekhad):** The Hebrew text uses a striking archival formula: "the king of [city], one" — repeated thirty-one times. The word 'ekhad ("one") serves as a tally mark, as if each conquered king is being checked off a list. In the Leningrad Codex, this section is written in a distinctive two-column layout — a visual feature unique to this passage, suggesting the scribes recognized its list character. PNEUMA preserves the "one" at the end of each entry with a dash to capture the cadence of a catalogue: not storytelling, but counting. The drumbeat of "one... one... one..." carries its own rhetoric — this is what thoroughness looks like.

**V. 18 — "Lasharon" (la-Sharron):** The Hebrew has the preposition la- (to/of) prefixed to Sharon, which is unusual. It may mean "of the Sharon plain" or may simply be the city's name with an embedded preposition. PNEUMA treats it as a place name, following the Masoretic vocalization.

**V. 23 — "Goiim at Gilgal" (goiim l'Gilgal):** The Hebrew goiim means "nations" — an unusual name for a kingdom. It may refer to a king who ruled over a mixed or multi-ethnic population, or "Goiim" may function as a place name (cf. Genesis 14:1, "Tidal king of Goiim"). The phrase has puzzled translators since antiquity; the LXX Vaticanus reflects a different reading. PNEUMA retains "Goiim" as a proper noun and lets the strangeness stand.

**V. 24 — "Thirty-one":** The explicit total matches the count of kings listed in verses 9-24. This is not decoration — it is verification. The number confirms the record is complete. Every throne has been accounted for.

**On the Vaticanus LXX:** The Greek text of Joshua 12 broadly follows the Hebrew, preserving the same structure and king-list. Variations are minor: some place names are transliterated differently, and occasional geographical phrases are expanded. The LXX confirms the antiquity and stability of this catalogue — it was already fixed in this form by the time of the Greek translation. PNEUMA follows the Masoretic text of the Leningrad Codex as its primary source.

— PNEUMA
